title: Johnny cakes
subtitle: Jamaican fried dumplings
excerpt: Small rounds of unsweetened dough fried in oil until the outside is deep brown and crisp and the inside is soft and doughy. Johnny cakes are the everyday Jamaican breakfast dumpling — served alongside ackee and saltfish, fried plantain, and callaloo. They take ten minutes. The dough is close to a very short bread dough: flour, baking powder, butter, and milk worked to a firm consistency.
sourceNotes: Johnny cakes (also spelled johnnycakes) appear across the Caribbean and in the American South with variations in recipe and name. The Jamaican version is an unsweetened fried dumpling, distinct from the sweetened cornmeal pancake of the American tradition. They are a staple of the Jamaican breakfast plate and appear in Jamaican culinary literature from the 19th century.

content.text: Johnny cake dough is stiffer than bread dough. It should not stick to your hands after a minute of kneading. If the dough feels tacky, add a small amount of flour. The resting time is worth taking — it relaxes the gluten and makes the dumplings softer inside.

Fry at a moderate heat — 160 to 170°C. Higher than this and the outside burns while the inside stays raw dough. The dumplings should bubble steadily and turn golden over 4 to 5 minutes on each side. Some Jamaicans cook them in just enough oil to shallow-fry; others deep-fry. Either works.

Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Rub in the {{unsalted-butter}} with your fingertips until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Add the {{whole-milk}} and mix to a firm, non-sticky dough. Knead for 1 minute, then rest for 5 minutes covered. Divide into 10 portions and roll each into a ball, then flatten slightly to about 1.5 cm thick. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} to 165°C. Fry in batches for 4 to 5 minutes per side until deep golden all over. Drain on kitchen paper. Eat immediately.

items.cause: Oil too hot or dumplings too thick
items.fix: Keep the oil at 165°C and flatten the dumplings to no more than 2 cm. Thick dumplings need a lower temperature and longer time
Add flour a tablespoon at a time until the dough stops sticking. The final dough should feel like firm bread dough

items.cause: Overworked dough or dough too dry
items.fix: Mix only until just combined and rest the dough before shaping. A short rest relaxes the gluten and gives a softer interior

content.text: Johnny cakes are the everyday Jamaican breakfast dumpling — the starchy element on the breakfast plate that anchors ackee and saltfish, callaloo, and fried plantain. They appear at the Jamaican table for any meal and as a snack. In the Jamaican diaspora in Britain they are made at home for weekend breakfasts and appear at Caribbean food events. The dish is resolutely practical: cheap, filling, made from pantry ingredients, and on the table in twenty minutes.
